coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots-based artificial tongue for determining and discriminating cigarettes
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Na
Wu, Qiuhua
Chen, Lijiang
Zhang, Guolin
Liu, Xue
coredata.dc:description: Smoking can cause cigarette-related diseases and pose serious threat to human health. Its dangers can be effectively controlled by discriminating cigarettes and monitoring cigarette quality. Herein, a kind of artificial tongue technique based on the indicator displacement assay (IDA) was developed and applied to determine and discriminate cigarettes and their main ingredients (saccharides, organic acids and nicotine). This method was constructed using carbon nanodots (CDs) as a fluorescent indicator and various concentrations of silver ion (Ag+) as a fluorescent regulator. A cigarette extracting solution was prepared to interact with an artificial tongue and produce fluorescence fingerprints. Twenty-nine kinds of cigarettes can be well discriminated in terms of category (flue-cured cigarette, blended cigarette and cigar), brand, origin (domestic or foreign cigarettes) after processing and visualizing the response fingerprints. The artificial tongue fluorescent sensor array can sensitively detect nine kinds of tobacco-based chemical ingredients and discriminate them between different concentrations. The as-prepared fluorescent artificial tongue is a promising platform for monitoring cigarette quality and controlling the harmful effects of smoking because of its cheap material requirements, simple operation, and good performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence detection
Cigarettes analysis
Sensor array
Carbon nanodots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the CDs. (B) UV–vis spectrum (the red curve), fluorescence excitation spectrum (the green curve) and fluorescence emission spectrum (the blue curve) of the CDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs; (D) XPS spectrum of the CDs. (E) Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of the CDs for C 1s. (F) Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of the CDs for N 1s (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Fingerprint of all the cigarettes investigated. Each value is the average of five independent measurements; each error bar shows the standard deviation of these measurements. (B) 3D PCA plot of the fluorescence modulation data obtained from the sensor array with all cigarettes investigated. Each point represents the response pattern for a single cigarette to the sensor array.
(A) 2D PCA plot of discriminating Zhongnanhai cigarettes according to their category. (B) HCA dendrogram of discriminating Zhongnanhai cigarettes according to their category.
Discrimination of the brand for three basic categories of cigarettes and the corresponding double-blind experiments: (A, D) Flue-cured cigarettes; (B, E) Blended cigarettes; (C, F) Cigars.

(A) Fingerprint of the tobacco-based analytes (50 μM). Each value is the average of five independent measurements; each error bar shows the standard deviation of these measurements. (B) 2D canonical score plot for response patterns obtained against 50 μM of the tobacco-based analytes using PCA.

(A) Fingerprint of the tobacco-based analytes and other interfering substances (all at 50 μM). Each value is the average of five independent measurements; each error bar shows the standard deviation of these measurements. (B) 2D canonical score plot for response patterns obtained against 50 μM of the tobacco-based analytes and other interfering substances using PCA.
Semiquantitative LDA plot of the tobacco-based analytes.
Detailed information of the investigated cigarettes used in this study.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots-based artificial tongue for determining and discriminating cigarettes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036 (P. R. China)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036 PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036 (P. R. China)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Smoking can cause cigarette-related diseases and pose serious threat to human health. Its dangers can be effectively controlled by discriminating cigarettes and monitoring cigarette quality. Herein, a kind of artificial tongue technique based on the indicator displacement assay (IDA) was developed and applied to determine and discriminate cigarettes and their main ingredients (saccharides, organic acids and nicotine). This method was constructed using carbon nanodots (CDs) as a fluorescent indicator and various concentrations of silver ion (Ag+) as a fluorescent regulator. A cigarette extracting solution was prepared to interact with an artificial tongue and produce fluorescence fingerprints. Twenty-nine kinds of cigarettes can be well discriminated in terms of category (flue-cured cigarette, blended cigarette and cigar), brand, origin (domestic or foreign cigarettes) after processing and visualizing the response fingerprints. The artificial tongue fluorescent sensor array can sensitively detect nine kinds of tobacco-based chemical ingredients and discriminate them between different concentrations. The as-prepared fluorescent artificial tongue is a promising platform for monitoring cigarette quality and controlling the harmful effects of smoking because of its cheap material requirements, simple operation, and good performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Cigarette smoking can cause serious health risks, including lung cancer, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, cardiovascular disease and peptic ulcer (Menvielle et al., 2018; Peto et al., 1999; Rees et al., 1982). Cigarette-related diseases are generally caused by poisonous chemicals in cigarette smoke (Ramdzan et al., 2018). Various brands and types of cigarettes are composed of tobacco with similar ingredients at different contents (e.g. saccharides, organic acids and nicotine), and a slight difference in cigarette ingredients can directly affect the toxic chemicals contained in cigarette smoke and the harmful effects of smoking (Baker et al., 2004; Talhout et al., 2006). Hence, an effective method for monitoring ingredients in cigarettes and discriminating various cigarettes should be developed (Sander et al., 2017).

Traditional analytical methods are unsuitable for analyzing cigarettes with complicated chemical ingredients. Based on mammalian olfactory and gustatory systems, sensor array techniques with signals from a group of sensors have been developed to recognize and discriminate analytes with similar structures or complicated compositions (Anzenbacher et al., 2010; Askim et al., 2013). In contrast to traditional specific “lock-and-key” recognition modes, array analysis is conducted through cumulative nonspecific responses from all sensors. Sample patterns generated by a sensor array are subsequently processed through a given algorithm to achieve results (Peveler et al., 2018). A sensor array can be divided into vapor-phase (artificial nose) and solution-phase (artificial tongue) sensor arrays based on sample state (Guerrini et al., 2017; Lavigne and Anslyn, 2001). Most of the current artificial nose/tongue sensing systems are applied to identify and differentiate a specific class of analytes (Albert et al., 2000; Bunz and Rotello, 2010; Diehl and Anslyn, 2013; Wright and Anslyn, 2006; Xu et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2015; Ahn et al., 2016; Abbasi-Moayed et al., 2018), such as metal ions, organic molecules, biological macromolecules, but are rarely used to analyze practical samples with complicated chemical ingredients, especially for some product samples such as cola, white wines and teas (Bender et al., 2018; Han et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017a; Han et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017b; Wang et al., 2018).

Using artificial nose/tongue systems based on sensor arrays to analyze cigarettes is rarely reported, and nearly all reports are based on artificial nose sensing systems (Brudzewski et al., 2012, 2010; Esteves et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2004; Ködderitzsch et al., 2005). Artificial nose sensing systems are usually constructed by combining a series of complicated components, including gas sensors, signal post-processing, and conditional control devices (pumps, flow controller, air conditioner, etc.) (Luo et al., 2004). Volatile components from cigarettes are collected, detected, and analyzed. However, two problems exist in current artificial nose sensing systems: a) sensing systems are complex in terms of their construction and operation; b) determination results are unstable and easily influenced by the external environment (temperature, humidity, etc.).

In comparison with artificial nose sensing systems, solution-based artificial tongue sensing systems are simple, and they do not involve any complicated conditional control devices. Their results are also accurate because of easily controlled test conditions (concentration, temperature, etc.). Herein, we have for the first time proposed a method for determining and discriminating cigarettes by using a solution-based artificial tongue sensing system based on the indicator displacement assay (IDA). A simple sensor platform was constructed with fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) as a single indicator and four concentrations of silver ions (Ag+) as regulators. CDs are a class of low-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which have aroused a great deal of interest in recent years (Hu et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019). A total of 145 cigarette samples within 29 species were recognized successfully in terms of their brands, categories, and origin via the sensing system. Nine main cigarette tobacco-related ingredients, including saccharides, organic acids, and nicotine (Nic), were also identified and distinguished.

Glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose, citric acid, malic acid, propandioic acid, oxalic acid, nicotine, cotinine (Cot), nornicotine (Nor) and anatabine (Ana) were obtained from InnoChem Science&Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). NaNO3, Na3PO4·12H2O, NH3·H2O, Pb(NO3)2, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O and CaCl2 were obtained from Titan Science&Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea and glycine were ordered from YongDa Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All the chemicals were analytical reagent, and we used them directly without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout all experiments. Dialysis membrane was ordered from Baoman Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The CDs were prepared through a facile microwave reaction according to our previous study (Wu et al., 2017), and the detailed preparation process is expounded in the ESI. The absorbance of CDs solution was regulated to 0.1 at their optimum excitation wavelength for minimizing the concentration effect of CDs. The sensor array was constructed through mixing different volumes (1 μL, 4 μL, 7 μL, 10 μL) of Ag+ solution (50 mM) with 5 mL of CDs solution respectively.

29 species of cigarette samples were purchased from local supermarkets in Shenyang, China. The cigarette extracting solution was prepared as follows: firstly, the cigarettes were dried in oven for 2 h at 35 °C; secondly, the cigarette tobacco was extracted, crashed into powders and sieved; thirdly, the tobacco powder (1 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (50 mL), and extracted with Soxhlet extractor for 1 h. The extracts were separated and diluted to 50 mL as final test samples. After that, a series of tobacco extract solutions with the same volume (5 μL) were added into the CD@Ag+ solutions, and the fluorescence spectra from the original CD solution, CD@Ag+ solution and CD@Ag+@extract solution were recorded.

A series of characterizations, including transmission electron microscope (TEM), Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), was performed to confirm the successful preparation of CDs, as well as investigate their basic chemical structure and composition. The CDs are spherical-like with a size of approximately 8 nm (Fig. 1 A) and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite carbon as shown in the high-resolution TEM image (Yang et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2017). Two obvious characteristic absorption peaks were detected at 210 and 330 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1B, the red curve), originating from the π−π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains (CC, CC) and the n−π* transition of multi-conjugated CO and CO (Miao et al., 2018). The optimum emission peak of CDs was centered at 380 nm with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 1B, the blue curve). The PL excitation spectrum (emission wavelength: 380 nm) of the CDs was close to their absorption band at 330 nm (Fig. 1B, the green curve), indicating that their emission originated from the n–π* transition of conjugated CO (Jian et al., 2017). The calculated fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs was 11.41% when quinine sulfate was used as a reference.

The elemental composition and surface functional state of the CDs were revealed through FT-IR and XPS. In Fig. 1C, the characteristic absorption band at about 3425 cm−1 could be ascribed to NH stretching vibration and −OH stretching vibration, and the characteristic bands of CN stretching vibration at 1410 and 1190 cm−1 were observed. The detailed analysis of the FT-IR spectrum suggested that CDs possessed rich nitrogen-containing functional groups. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) showed three strong peaks at 287.4, 400.8 and 532.0 eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The deconvoluted high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) showed five peaks at 284.4, 285.6, 286.5, 288.7 and 289.5 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO, CO, and −COOH groups, respectively. The deconvoluted high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) exhibited three fitted peaks at 399.6, 400.3 and 401.2 eV, which were associated with amino N groups, pyrrolic-like N and N in a graphite-like structure, respectively. The C 1s and N 1s spectra could further confirm the rich nitrogen resources in the CDs and show the diversity of nitrogen-containing functional groups.

Tobacco has three key chemical ingredients, including saccharides, organic acids and Nic (Torikaiu et al., 2005; Weeks, 1985). In this study, sensing materials, which are sensitive to the three ingredients, were designed to discriminate various cigarettes more effectively. However, an IDA was introduced to the design of sensing systems instead because of the complicated design and preparation process. In contrast to traditional specific sensors, an IDA assay allocates the sensing and recognition functions to indicators and receptors, respectively, thereby reducing difficulties in designing and preparing sensors (Schiller et al., 2007; Buryak et al., 2007; Buryak and Severin, 2004). In this study, given the unique affinity of the N- or O-containing functional groups of tobacco-based chemical ingredients to specific metal ions (Shoeib et al., 2002, 2001; Turel, 2002), Ag+ was chosen and utilized as the receptor. The corresponding Ag+-responsive CDs were synthesized to construct the IDA-based sensor array (Figure S-1 and Figure S-2). Ag+ can chelate with N- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs and quench the fluorescence of CDs by facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process (Jiang et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2013; Wang and Ding, 2014). The zeta potential of the CDs shifted from -8.51 to 4.43 mV when 1.4 mM Ag+ was added, demonstrating that Ag+ was adsorbed on their surface (Figure S-3). The fluorescence lifetime of CD@1.4 mM Ag+ (3.54 ns) was clearly shorter than that of the CDs (4.65 ns) (Figure S-4). This finding was consistent with previous reports (Lakowicz, 2006; Bao et al., 2011; Gao et al., 2015; Zu et al., 2017) and showed an obvious photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between CDs and Ag+. Four optimal Ag+ concentrations (10 μM, 40 μM, 70 μM, 100 μM) were selected from the concentration range of the linear response of the CDs to Ag+ (0–100 μM) to construct the CD@Ag+ sensing system. In this way, a solid detection result could be guaranteed. Upon the addition of cigarette tobacco-based analytes and cigarette samples, the fluorescence intensities were either recovered or further quenched to different degrees because of the competing combination with Ag+ between analytes and CDs. An artificial tongue sensing system was then established on the basis of these characteristics.

Table 1 displays the detailed information of all the cigarettes selected for the current study. The fluorescence response patterns obtained from a matrix (4 sensors × 29 analytes × 5 repetitions) were recorded and normalized to generate the fingerprint of the cigarettes (Fig. 2 A). The relative fluorescence change values in the fingerprint were further statistically analyzed through principal component analysis (PCA), which is a standard tool of multivariate statistical analysis (Askim et al., 2013). The 3D PCA plot depicted the overall results of the discrimination experiments, and all the cigarettes were easily differentiated (Fig. 2B). Three principal components (contributing over 90%) were taken and applied to differentiate all the samples with 100% accuracy. As shown in the Fig. 2B, the distribution of the cigarette data points belonging to the same category was relatively concentrated. Flue-cured cigarettes were located in the left region of the scatter plot, whereas blended cigarettes and cigars were found in the center and right regions of the scatter plot. This result indicated that the category of cigarettes could be well identified in the PCA plot. Six cigarettes belonging to two categories from the same brand (Zhongnanhai cigarettes) were chosen and analyzed with the sensor array through PCA and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) to receive more visualized results about category division. The 2D PCA plot (Fig. 3 A) showed that cigarette samples under the same category were clustered together. A consistent result was also observed using the k-nearest neighbor in the HCA dendrogram (Fig. 3B). Six cigarettes were categorized into two groups, and uncertainties or errors in clustering were not observed in five trials. In comparison with the PCA plot, the HCA dendrogram provided more details about the relationship between diverse cigarettes and their similarities. Smoking is hazardous to health, and its damage extent can be affected by the type of cigarettes. For example, the hazard indices of flue-cured cigarettes were lower than those of blended cigarettes (Xia et al., 2012). These results showed that the CD@Ag+ sensing system could excellently determine and discriminate cigarettes, even without any detailed product information. In comparison with previously reported artificial nose sensing systems for cigarettes (Brudzewski et al., 2012, 2010; Esteves et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2004), the facile artificial tongue sensing system applied the least sensing units and detected the most cigarette brands (Table S-8).

In addition to distinguishing different cigarettes and showing the category of cigarettes, classifying cigarettes into various brands could be achieved by using the CD@Ag+ sensing system. Cigarettes from the same category were further analyzed with the as-prepared sensor array. In the 2D PCA plot obtained from the sensor array (Fig. 4 A, B and C), each cigarette was successfully discriminated, and all the cigarettes from the same brand clustered together, such as Zhongnanhai cigarettes. In the PCA plot of the flue-cured and blended cigarettes, domestic and foreign cigarettes were also clearly distinguished. The category of cigarettes (flue-cured cigarettes, blended cigarettes and cigar) mainly corresponds to the manufacturing technology of tobacco such as toasted method, air-cured & toasted method, and air-cured & fermented method (Table 1), and various processing technologies may affect the changes in the composition of tobacco (Sander et al., 2017). Apart from general manufacturing technology, typical processing technologies for cigarette tobacco are used by different manufactures. Breeds and production areas also affect the ingredients and tastes of tobacco. All these details would determine the final effective discriminative results of the as-prepared IDA-based sensor array.

Double-blind experiments were performed to evaluate the accuracy and practicability of the sensor array. Three cigarette samples were randomly selected from the previous training samples. The fluorescence response was recorded and analyzed with PCA based on the initial training matrix. All the blind samples were correctly assigned to the respective corresponding cigarettes on the basis of the shortest Mahalanobis distance (Fig. 4D, E and F).

The sensing system was further applied to analyze the tobacco-based analytes and confirm the analysis mechanism of the as-constructed sensor array in relation to the main ingredients. As mentioned above, saccharides, organic acids, and Nic are three key chemical ingredients that determine the quality and characteristic flavor of cigarettes. Among all the saccharides and organic acids in cigarette tobacco, four kinds of saccharides, including glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc) and maltose (Mal), and four kinds of organic acids, including citric acid (CA), malic acid (MA), propandioic acid (PA) and oxalic acid (OA), contribute the most to the quality and characteristic flavor of cigarettes (Talhout et al., 2006; Rodgman, 2002; Leffingwell, 1999). Hence, four saccharides (Glu, Fru, Suc and Mal), four organic acids (CA, MA, PA and OA) and Nic were grouped together and treated with the sensor array. These nine principal ingredients of cigarette tobacco with the same concentration (50 μM) were added to CD@Ag+ solutions, and fluorescence change was acquired and normalized to form the fingerprint (Fig. 5 A). The response data sets obtained through the matrix (4 sensors × 9 analytes × 5 repetitions) were statistically visualized through PCA. As shown in Fig. 5B, all the analytes were correctly classified, and saccharides and organic acids were distributed in the left and right regions of the graph, respectively. Discrimination capability relies on different Ag+-related action sites and action modes from various saccharides, organic acids, and Nic. Organic acids with −COOH functional groups and Nic with N-containing functional groups can take Ag+ away from CDs and recover the fluorescence of CDs to a certain extent (Tan et al., 2011). Conversely, saccharides with multiple hydroxyl groups can bridge two silver centers of CD@Ag+ through weak chelation and quench the fluorescence of CDs (Nguyen and Anslyn, 2006). With the competing interaction of Ag+, CDs, and analytes, the sensing system showed differential response signals to various analytes and enabled all analytes to be well distinguished and detected. The ability to excellently discriminate saccharides, organic acids and nicotine provided indirect proof supporting the proposed cigarette determination strategy.

The selectivity and anti-interference capability of the sensing system were further investigated by introducing other tobacco-based chemical compounds (Cot, Nor, Ana, NO3 −, PO4 3−, NH3·H2O, Pb2+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, all with 50 μM) to the sensing platform as interfering substances (Leffingwell, 1999). Fig. 6 A showed that the sensor array had no obvious response to NO3 −, PO4 3−, NH3·H2O, Pb2+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+, but to Cot, Nor and Ana. Although these alkaloids could induce array responses, they were obviously separated from Nic and other analytes. Fig. 6B illustrated intuitively the response of the sensor array to various analytes. It is interesting to note that Nic, Cot, Nor and Ana were concentrated in specific areas of the figure and separated from one another. This observation might be attributed to the similarity of their structure to that of Nic, and their ability to coordinate with Ag+, inducing similar changes in the formation of CD@Ag+ aggregates (Lisko et al., 2013). However, other functional groups in these molecules may result in differences in affinities and cause variation in fluorescence signals. These results revealed the excellent recognition ability and selectivity of the as-developed artificial tongue for saccharides, organic acids, and alkaloids in tobacco.

A semiquantitative analysis was carried out to identify tobacco-based analytes at various concentrations and to further illustrate the ability of the sensor array to recognize the key chemical ingredients of tobacco. The recorded fluorescence response data were analyzed through linear discriminant analysis (LDA) (Fig. 7 ). These results indicated that the fluorescence response was significantly dependent on the concentrations of analytes. The data points of all the analytes were distributed in the central region of the scattered plot at a low concentration, and an obvious outward diffusion was observed as the analyte concentration increased. The classification of the 230 data points (9 analytes + control, 5 repetitions per sample) was 100% correct, indicating the excellent recognition capability of the sensor array. These results validated the practicability of the developed artificial-tongue sensing system for monitoring cigarette quality.

In conclusion, a new IDA-based CD@Ag+ artificial tongue was created and applied to detect and discriminate cigarettes. Twenty-nine kinds of cigarettes were successfully discriminated and classified on the basis of their categories, brands, and origin (domestic or foreign cigarettes). For uncertain cigarette samples, their manufacturing technology and production area could be pre-determined simply on the basis of the as-constructed PCA plots. The determination mechanism of the sensor array originated from the effective analysis of the main tobacco ingredients, including saccharides, organic acids, and Nic. The accurate qualitative and semiquantitative analysis of the main saccharides, organic acids, and Nic not only confirmed the analysis mechanism of the developed sensor array in relation to the main ingredients but also indicated the practicability of the sensor array in the quality control of cigarettes. This research exploited a simple and accurate method for monitoring the quality of cigarettes and could provide a certain health guide that would help smokers choose appropriate cigarettes.
These authors contributed equally to the publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China [51873085], Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province of China [20180510023 and 20180550947], Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China [LYB201603] and Shenyang Municipal Program for the Top Young and Middle-aged Innovative Talents of Liaoning Province of China [RC170258].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121118.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Abbasi-Moayed et al, 2018]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A nanopaper-based artificial tongue: a ratiometric fluorescent sensor array on bacterial nanocellulose for chemical discrimination applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Menvielle, I. Kulhánová, J. Bryère, G. Launoy, D. Eilstein, C. Delpierre and I. Soerjomataram, Tobacco-Attributable Burden of Cancer According to Socioeconomic Position in France, Inter. J. Cancer., 143 (2018) 478−485.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Ahn et al, 2016]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Duplex bioelectronic tongue for sensing umami and sweet tastes based on human taste receptor nanovesicles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Peto, Z. M. Chen and J. Boreham, Tobacco−the Growing Epidemic, Nat. Med., 5 (1999) 15.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Albert et al, 2000]
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. J. Rees, P. J. Chowienczyk, J. G. Ayres and T. J. H. Clark, Irritant Effects of Cigarette and Cigar Smoke, Lancet., 320 (1982) 1015−1017.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Anzenbacher et al, 2010]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A practical approach to optical cross-reactive sensor arrays chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. N. Ramdzan, M. I. G. S. Almeida, M. J. McCullough, M. A. Segundo and S. D. Kolev, Determination of Salivary Cotinine as Tobacco Smoking Biomarker, TrAC-Trend. Anal. Chem., 105 (2018) 89−97.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Askim et al, 2013]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical sensor arrays for chemical sensing: the optoelectronic nose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. R. Baker, J. R. Pereira da Silva and G. Smith, The Effect of Tobacco Ingredients on Smoke Chemistry. Part I: Flavourings and Additives, Food Chem. Toxicol., 42 (2004) 3−37.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Baker et al, 2004]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of tobacco ingredients on smoke chemistry. Part I: flavourings and additives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Talhout, A. Opperhuizen and J. G. C. van Amsterdam, Sugars as Tobacco Ingredient: Effects on Mainstream Smoke Composition, Food Chem. Toxicol., 44 (2006) 1789−1798.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Bao et al, 2011]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. C. Sander, J. S. Pritchett, Y. C. Daniels, L. J. Wood, B. E. Lang and S. A. Wise, etc., Development of a Cigarette Tobacco Filler Standard Reference Material, Anal. Chem., 89 (2017) 10461−10467.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Bender et al, 2018]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Immobilized poly(aryleneethynylene) pH-strips discriminate different brands of cola

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. P. Anzenbacher, P. Lubal, P. Buček, M. A. Palacios and M. E. Kozelkova, A Practical Approach to Optical Cross-Reactive Sensor Arrays Chem. Soc. Rev., 39 (2010) 3954−3979.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Brudzewski et al, 2010]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Differential electronic nose of two chemo sensor arrays for odor discrimination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. R. Askim, M. Mahmoudi and K. S. Suslick, Optical Sensor Arrays for Chemical Sensing: The Optoelectronic Nose, Chem. Soc. Rev., 42 (2013) 8649−8682.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Brudzewski et al, 2012]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Differential electronic nose and support vector machine for fast recognition of tobacco

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. J. Peveler, R. F. Landis, M. Yazdani, J. W. Day, R. Modi and C. J. Carmalt, etc., A Rapid and Robust Diagnostic for Liver Fibrosis Using a Multichannel Polymer Sensor Array, Adv. Mater., 30 (2018) 1800634.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Bunz and Rotello, 2010]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticle–fluorophore complexes: sensitive and discerning “Noses” for biosystems sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Guerrini, E. Garcia-Rico, N. Pazos-Perez and R.A. Alvarez-Puebla, Smelling, Seeing, Tasting−Old Senses for New Sensing, ACS Nano., 11 (2017) 5217−5222.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Buryak and Severin, 2004]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An organometallic chemosensor for the sequence-selective detection of histidine- and methionine-containing peptides in water at neutral pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. J. Lavigne and E.V. Anslyn, Sensing A Paradigm Shift in the Field of Molecular Recognition: From Selective to Differential Receptors, Angew. Chem. Int. Edit., 40 (2001) 3118−3130.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Buryak et al, 2007]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pattern-based sensing of nucleotides in aqueous solution with a multicomponent Indicator displacement assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. J. Albert, N. S. Lewis, C. L. Schauer, G. A. Sotzing, S. E. Stitzel and T. P. Vaid, etc., Cross-Reactive Chemical Sensor Arrays, Chem. Rev., 100 (2000) 2595−2626.
